sentences.text: Yes, you--
Yeah.
Well, I just want to tell you that being locked up in a little apartment for eight months-- I think I was out maybe three or four times.
In the evening, the boys would take me out for an airing a couple-- a few times. 'Tedious' is the word.
The stress is much less, and the boredom is much more.
So-- but that this is, again, a sort of a-- a quiet interval.
Of course, when my father came-- he had survived the first attack and the first uprising of the ghetto of Warsaw and came out after the first uprising.
So when he came, he told us about it and how terrible it was.
And there was an underground paper that the young men would bring.
Both of them were involved with the underground, as were most young-- young men in Poland.
And they knew [? when ?] the ghetto was destroyed.
They told us about it, but they didn't tell us the details, out of pity for us or compassion.
At the end of the eight months we were running out of money.
And obviously, they could not support us.
We didn't pay them an exorbitant amount, but we had to support ourselves.
We had to pay them rent.
We heard, via the-- the grapevine, through their nephew, the young-- the man that my father had contacted, that a very peculiar thing was happening in Warsaw.
There was no ghetto of Warsaw anymore.
There were no official Jews alive.
But all of a sudden, the Germans were negotiating through Switzerland, with-- with the Swiss, for some trucks and other supplies that they by then needed rather badly.
This was in the spring of 1943.
That they were willing to-- to let out some Jews who were relatives of some people in South America.
A hotel called Hotel Polski was assigned for the meeting and negotiating place for this exchange.
And this hotel became off limits to the Gestapo, in the sense everybody was safe who came to that hotel.
And Jews who heard about-- of course, the families of these people from South America were no longer alive.
So these were papers which had just names on them, no pictures.
So the German SS, the German Gestapo, and the Jewish Gestapo were selling places on these lists to-- to be members of these families, to whatever survivors showed up at the Hotel Polski.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We didn't have enough money to get on those lists, as it turned out luckily, because nobody survive.

But they also had a list which was cheaper because it seemed to be less official, so to speak, of people, Jews, who had relatives in-- in, at the time, Palestine, relatives who were willing to take them in.
And of course, we legitimately did have such relatives in Palestine.
So we decided to try this because we did-- you know, you didn't know how long the war would last.
As a matter of fact, it always seemed to me that it would never end.
It just seemed that way till the very end.
Sure.
Yes.
No.
It was really not a possib-- well, the two young men in the-- in that apartment, I remember flirting a little bit with one of them and he with me.
He was the one who wanted to become a priest, by the way, so-- [LAUGHS] but nothing really serious.
And I will tell you when-- when I tell you about the camp.
Yes, there was some-- not dating, you know.
But-- but some flirting and so on, a little bit.
Yeah, sure.
Well, you know, in the ghetto of Warsaw, when it existed, there was a Jewish government called the Judenrat.
Had always been-- the Jewish community had its own sort of-- not jurisdiction in Poland, but it had a-- a-- a an administrative unit.
And the-- the Germans maintained it and communicated with the Jewish populations through that.
There was a Jewish police.
And there were people, Jews, who were willing to denounce other Jews to save their own lives.
And a little unit was formed, which was called the Jewish Gestapo, a kind of an adjunct to the German Gestapo.
And these people survived in larger numbers the destruction of the ghetto than others, of course.
And they showed up at this Hotel Polski.
None of them survived, by the way.
And they had enough confidence in the project to enlist themselves, you know, put themselves on the lists, and went with us, as it turned out, to a German camp.
So in this Hotel Polski, we were there a few days.
And from there, we were transported by train into what they called a staging place, which turned out the German camp of Bergen-Belsen-- B-E-R-G-E-N, dash, B-E-L-Z-E-N.
And that camp was in the northwest of Germany.
The nearest city, big city, was Hamburg.
How long a trip was it?
A couple of days on the train and, as a matter of fact, passenger train, of all things.
On the train-- I really don't remember too much.
Well, a couple of days was not really-- no, [? really, ?] it was not bad.
It was not bad.
We were, at the time-- apparently still they were thinking there would be an exchange.
When we got to Bergen-Belsen, we were put-- even though it was a big camp, usually about 20,000 inmates all together at one time, it was not an extermination camp.
It didn't have gas chambers.
We were put as a separate group.
Different groups were kept separate from other groups by barbed wires.
And our group remained together for the duration.
I was there for about 22 months.
The group started about 3,500 people.
As soon as we got there, the-- the-- the-- apparently the exchange fell through.
Of course, I don't know the details of this.
But the Germans sent a high up Gestapo officer, whose name I don't remember, from Berlin.
No, I never saw him.
He interrogated heads of families and a couple of members of the family.
So I was never personally interrogated by him.
My father was.
And he, of course, immediately discovered the fraud of this-- these so-called families that would have, like, 35 members, where sometimes the children would be older than the parents.
It was all done very haphazardly.
And after about three months, about 2,900 people were removed, all the members of these South American papers.
As we later found out, they had been-- they were transported to Auschwitz and gassed.
The remaining group-- there were two groups.
One was this Palestinian list.
And the other was also South American papers, but very legitimate, people who really had relatives in either United States or South America, and really were the people who were on the lists.
And none of them were from Warsaw.
They were all from [?
Lwow, ?] L-W-O-W, also known as Lemberg.
And there were about half and half.
Half-- we were about 250, and they were about 350 people.
So 600 people remained.
We were, to the end, a somewhat privileged group, I don't think for any reason other than sort of administrative continuity.
The Germans were always obsessed with formality, even in a camp.
For example, when people died, my father, who had to make out the death certificate, would have to write that they had a heart attack, you know, or something.
Even if you knew that they died of starvations, you were not allowed to write anything like that.
They kept records.
He was the camp doctor in our group.
You see, at the beginning, there were several doctors in the group.
Later on, there were just two.
And my father was selected by the Germans to be the representative of-- of our group.
And what made us a select group is that we never worked.
And this had pluses and minuses.
The pluses definitely were more important, because it meant, number one, that we did not use up as much energy.
The food that they gave us was very scant.

We received some ersatz coffee in the morning, a soup in the middle of the day, a quart of soup, which was almost invariably turnip soap with little pieces of fish sometimes floating and little pieces of potato, and more coffee in the evening.
And once or twice a week, bread soup in the evening, a horrible thing which tasted wonderful at the time.
And we also were a group, a mixed group, men and women together.
As a matter of fact, at the beginning, we had to sleep separately.
And later on, it didn't make any difference, because when you have so little food one of the things that disappears is interest in any sex.
We had one child who was born from early encounters in the ghetto who survived and, with his mother, went on to Israel.
And I know they survived in Israel.
Her name was Tomkiewicg.
I don't remember her first name.
T-O-M-K-I-E-W-I-C-G.
It was boy child, yes, a baby, and he survived.
Oh, I don't remember.
I don't remember at all.
Well, it must have been her husband, I imagine.
But maybe he was even conceived before, because I don't remember the father in the camp, and I know that only she survived with the child.
Anyway, after we became a group of 600, we were allowed to be together, mixed men and women.
My father worked as a doctor, and he sort of apprenticed me, as I was learning to become a nurse.
His duties were to-- to negotiate with the German doctor for the little bit of maybe aspirin or bandages or other things, even a little bit of sulfa drugs that they would give us.
His duties were to decide who was allowed, who was sick and, therefore, was allowed not to come out for the roll call.
Because every morning there would be a roll call.
You had to come out and stand in rows and be counted.
And the counting would be sometimes a perfunctory affair, taking a few minutes.
And sometimes, if for a good or bad reason or whatever, the-- the soldier or officer doing it, usually a noncom sort of, we would be left standing there for half a day, or even a day.
So my father ministered as much as he could to the sick in our group.
And he--
Only our group.
Yes.
Our group was privileged in another way.
At the beginning, the-- the people who were our official representatives with the Germans were the-- the Jewish Gestapo, who, of course, continued-- this-- this was customary in-- in German camps.

And you probably know the name kapo, which is a sort of a-- a-- a-- a-- like a foreman, you know, an inmate of the camp who led the privileged life and who was usually absolutely the dregs of society, because he was willing to-- to-- to keep his life better by oppressing his own people.

We asked the-- the German, which was very risky, the German commander of the camp-- I think his name was Hess, H-E-S-S, one of those people who was later in the Nuremberg Trials-- if he would allow us to pick our own representative and govern ourselves.
And again, he was intrigued by it and permitted it, so that our leader became a wonderful man and engineer who spoke beautiful, perfect German.
His name was Solowiejczik, S-O-L-O-W-I-E-J-C-Z-I-K.
And within our group, we were governed in a very fair way.
For example, you know, when a group works together, it can be better for everybody.
We would-- he would, for example, negotiate with the German guards to get some milk or some other food, which we would deprive ourselves of so the children would have it or the sick people.
And it gave us a sense of being human.
One of the things that happened to people in camps is-- it's the same psychology as slaves in the United States.
When you're oppressed enough, many people believe that you deserve it.
It's like abused women and children who feel that they deserve it.
The people themselves come to think of themselves as being sub-human.
This did not happen to us, I think, largely because we were a mixed group and a group that could maintain an internal dignity because we treated each other humanely.
Occasionally, you know, there would be beatings on a completely random basis.
As I told you, that was one of the advantages of not working.
By not working, we were less in daily contact with the German guards, who, again, behaved in a very capricious fashion.
Some of the guards, by the way, were women.
Some of the women-- our group had some children who survived in a mixed group.
Some of the women would pick one child to whom they would bring toys, their favorite, their pet child, you know.
Their maternal instinct-- instinct and their brutal instincts, they managed to keep separated, you know.
At one point, for example, we were next to a group of Albanian Jews.
And one of my friends met a young man-- you asked if there were flirtations-- met a young man, a young, Albanian man.
They fell in love.
They were separated at the end of the war.
They managed to find each other after the war.
They were married, and they lived in Venezuela together.
He was a-- a hotel manager, and she was a-- a-- a department store manager.
I know-- [LAUGHS], you know.
So there were some.
There were good friendships, deep friendships formed.
For example, I was very friendly with two women who had-- who were extremely friendly, close to each other, because they had survived the same tragedy.
They had both lost a child in the ghetto of Warsaw during the deportations.
And they both felt guilty, because when we were in that little enclave, they walked out with papers and left their children hidden, and they never saw their children again.
And they were both overcome with guilt, which created a very strong bond between them.
Yes, in training to become a nurse.
I gave injections, and I learned whatever.
My father was such a believer in education that-- under the worst possible circumstances.
And I think this is very good because it makes your life a little more normal when you try to think about other things.
You know, I learned English in the concentration camp.
For a piece of bread, we bought a copy of And we had a friend in the camp who spoke English, and he taught me English using I had a very Southern point of view on the Civil War at the time.
And-- but surprisingly enough, when the war ended, I could speak English.
In the-- in the camp, the days were, for us, were really monotonous.
In the morning, we lived on berths, very crowded, in one large room, maybe 100 or 150 people in one large room.
In the morning, my father would come, and I-- I was always on the top berth-- there were three levels-- because I was young.
I could climb.
He would come, and I would shake out my straw pillow and my blanket to throw out the mice that literally bred in my bed, you know.
I have a horror of rodents ever since then, understandably.
Then we would have something to eat.
And then they would-- I would do the rounds with my father in the morning.
Then we would have the-- the-- the soup, and then would be the whole afternoon till the next roll call and evening coffee with nothing to do, and then the whole evening with nothing to do.
People who could tell stories, who could summarize, dramatize books, narrators, were in great demand, to try to spend the time, take your mind off your constant hunger.
I did not feel full for two years.
Not once.
No, I didn't.
I was bloated actually.
Some people became very thin, and I looked very healthy, as a matter of fact.
But it was really kind of a bloat, which I lost eventually later.
I have a predisposition for being rather heavy, [LAUGHS], which is--
It is-- it is genetic.
So-- but anyway, occasionally we would manage to get hold of a German newspaper in German, and that would be an occasion for the camp in the evening to come together.
And some people who spoke good German would read-- read it, and they would translate it.
And they would-- you'd read between the lines because, of course, the Germans never retreated.
They only tightened the-- the lines, et cetera.
But these people who knew the geography of-- of Europe very well could tell from the names of the towns how the war was going, you know.
Things did not change very much during the 22 months.
The big change was that after a year, the Germans started deporting our group again.
And they deported the people from Lwow alphabetically-- no, all of us, excuse me, alphabetically.
They deported 150 the first week, 150 the next week, and then they stopped.
We never really found out till after the war what had happened.
We were S, so we-- we stayed.
What happened was that there was one man in the camp who was a butcher but had a name, the same name, as a very famous Jewish professor.
I don't remember the name.
He got legitimately exchanged.
I mean, the professor was being exchanged.
But the Germans, probably with their sort of black sense of humor, exchanged this butcher who was-- had been in our camp, went to Switzerland with the list of people in the camp hidden.
And in 1944 when it happened, the Swiss said, all these people have really friends and so on, and maybe we'll exchange something.
And the Germans stopped the deportations.
I believe 342 people survived.
My mother was not one of them.
My mother died in the camp of ileitis.
She became ill.
They thought it was her gallbladder because it was very similar symptoms.
And my father at the time-- at the time, the German doctor was a very decent man who literally extended to my father professional courtesy.
I don't remember.
But they took my mother out of the camp to the nearest military hospital, operated on her, and she died on the operating table.
They brought the body back, so that I can tell you that they really did the-- the surgery.
They didn't just kill her.
And they allowed us to go through all the rituals, and then they took the body and burned it because there was, of course, no-- no room.
I have to tell you that other groups, of course, did not fare as well as we did in that camp.
We knew that.
At one point, we were in a cell.
I don't know what you would call it.
It was a barrack with large rooms.
And the room next to us was a kind of a-- a-- a-- an exterminating room, where people would be crowded, like 800 to 1,000, literally stacked one on top of the other, locked up.
The windows would be locked up, and they would be sort of suffocating in there.
And we would hear the cries during the night in every European language of people in extremis.
And in the morning-- and they would put a few kapos in there to-- to beat them and stomp, and people would panic.
There wouldn't be enough air, and they would literally kick each other and-- and stomp on each other.
And they would bring out the bodies in the morning, you know.
So even though there were no gas chambers, there were terrible things going on in Bergen-Belsen also-- Bergen-Belsen, you know, the place where Anne Frank died.
She was in a-- in a-- in a group next to ours.
I never met her, but we knew there were Dutch and Hungarian Jews in the next group next to us.
We knew from the papers and so on that-- that the war, the Germans were losing the war.
We could not visualize how we would survive the end of the war.
The way it-- it finally happened is that one day, the Germans showed up, again with their dogs, and took us to the same siding.
We had to march several kilometers to the siding, a group of 5,000 Jews.
And they told us that we were being transferred to Theresienstadt.
We knew that Bergen-Belsen was in the path of the British troops.
This was now in April of 1945.
And they put us on a train, 5,000 of us.
The train was equipped with an anti-aircraft gun, and we were traveling through Germany going east.
We were on that train for about 10 days.
No, I'm wrong.
April 3 to April 13 we were on the train.
April 13 we were liberated.
So on that train for 10 days they never fed us.
The only food we got was at the various little stations where we stopped, young people would run off the train.
And there were always stacks of turnips and beets or potatoes lying in the train station, and we would grab some.
No cooking facilities, eating raw.
The last few days, standing near the river Elbe and running off the train, begging for food in surrounding villages.
Then we were liberated.
